---
title: "Baltimore"
description: "Baltimore — FYS: Progress"
---

<!-- ── Page Header ────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<header style="padding-block: var(--sp-2xl); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule);">
  <div class="container--text">
    <p class="hero__eyebrow">AS.001.219 · Baltimore as Classroom</p>
    <h1>Progress in the City</h1>
    <p class="lead">
      In this course we reflect on what it means to think about progress in Baltimore —
      a city where the history of economic growth, exclusion, and ongoing change is never
      far from the surface.
    </p>
  </div>
</header>

<!-- ── Map ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section>
  <div class="container">
    <div id="map"></div>
    <p class="small text-mid mt-md">
      Click a marker to learn about each site. Sites marked <span style="color: var(--ink-mid); font-weight: 600;">Class visit</span> are part of planned course field trips and are not available for the Baltimore Video assignment. All other sites are available for groups to choose.
    </p>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Site List ──────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section style="background: var(--bg-alt);">
  <div class="container">
    <h2 class="mb-lg">Sites</h2>
    <p style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.85rem; color: var(--ink-mid); margin-bottom: var(--sp-lg); padding: var(--sp-md); background: var(--accent-pale); border-left: 4px solid var(--accent); border-radius: 2px;">
      <strong>Note:</strong> This list of locations and their descriptions is preliminary and subject to change, including in response to feedback from local communities and organizations. Sites labeled "Class visit" are reserved for course field trips and are not available for the Baltimore Video assignment.
    </p>
    <h3 style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 600; letter-spacing: 0.08em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--ink-light); margin-bottom: var(--sp-md);">Class visits — not available for video assignment</h3>
    <div class="module-grid" style="margin-bottom: var(--sp-xl);">

      <div class="module-card" style="opacity: 0.8;">
        <div class="module-card__number" style="color: var(--ink-light);">Class visit</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Baltimore Museum of Industry</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          Inner Harbor. Baltimore's industrial revolution — canning, garment manufacture,
          printing. The BMI documents how ordinary workers built the modern economy.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions" style="color: var(--ink-mid); font-weight: 600;">Reserved for class field trip</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card" style="opacity: 0.8;">
        <div class="module-card__number" style="color: var(--ink-light);">Class visit</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Walters Art Museum</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          Mount Vernon. One of the great encyclopedic art museums in the United States,
          built on a private collection donated to the city. Art, patronage, and the
          question of who gets to define cultural progress.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions" style="color: var(--ink-mid); font-weight: 600;">Reserved for class field trip (Renaissance session)</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card" style="opacity: 0.8;">
        <div class="module-card__number" style="color: var(--ink-light);">Class visit (TBC)</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Manufactured Housing Factory</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          Baltimore area (TBC). A factory producing manufactured homes — industrial
          production, housing affordability, and what it means to build progress
          at scale for ordinary people.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions" style="color: var(--ink-mid); font-weight: 600;">Pending confirmation</div>
      </div>

    </div>

    <h3 style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.75rem; font-weight: 600; letter-spacing: 0.08em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--ink-light); margin-bottom: var(--sp-md);">Available for the Baltimore Video assignment</h3>
    <div class="module-grid">

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 1</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Frederick Douglass–Isaac Myers Maritime Park</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          Fells Point. Abolition, Black entrepreneurship, and the history of labor rights.
          What progress has looked like for Black Americans in Baltimore — and how far it
          has and hasn't come.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Race &amp; progress · entrepreneurship</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 2</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Johns Hopkins Hospital &amp; Medical Campus</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          East Baltimore. Medical science as an engine of progress — and the complicated
          history of how the campus was built, who it displaced, and who it serves.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Medical innovation · public health</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 3</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Science + Technology Park at Hopkins</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          East Baltimore. A biotech knowledge cluster built in one of Baltimore's most
          disadvantaged neighborhoods. Urban renewal, the knowledge economy, and the
          question of who gains from a city's reinvention.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Knowledge economy · urban change</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 4</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Port of Baltimore</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          Dundalk / Seagirt Marine Terminal. Global trade and logistics — the material
          infrastructure of progress. A story of deindustrialization, automation, and
          what happens to workers when the economy moves on.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Global trade · deindustrialization</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 5</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Enoch Pratt Free Library</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          Central Branch, Cathedral Street. One of America's first free public library
          systems. The politics of who gets access to knowledge — and how that has changed.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Information access · public goods</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 6</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Lexington Market</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          West Baltimore. One of America's oldest continuously operating public markets.
          Urban markets, entrepreneurship, food systems, and neighborhood change over
          nearly 250 years.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Markets · urban history</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 7</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Maryland Institute College of Art (MICA)</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          Mount Royal Avenue. Creative economy and design innovation. How do arts
          institutions shape what counts as progress? Groups choosing this site
          may want to pair it with the Baltimore Museum of Art (Site 8).
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Creative economy · design</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 8</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Baltimore Museum of Art (BMA)</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          North Charles Street, adjacent to the JHU Homewood campus. A civic art
          institution built on patronage and endowment. How do cities invest in
          cultural progress — and who defines what that means? Consider pairing
          with MICA (Site 7) for a richer comparison.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Cultural institutions · patronage · civic progress</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 9</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Inner Harbor / Harborplace</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          From industrial waterfront to tourist destination — and now in flux again.
          A case study in urban renewal, deindustrialization, and the question of
          who a city is rebuilt for.
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Urban renewal · tourism · reinvention</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 10</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Port Covington / Sagamore Development</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          South Baltimore. One of the largest private urban development projects in
          American history. What does 21st-century urban progress look like — and
          who decides?
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Contemporary urbanism · private investment</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 11</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Sandtown-Winchester</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          West Baltimore. In the 1990s, developer James Rouse led a $130M public-private
          initiative to rebuild this neighborhood from the ground up. The results were
          deeply mixed. Why do well-funded, well-intentioned efforts to create progress
          sometimes fail — and what can we learn from that?
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Attempted progress · community reinvestment · what goes wrong</div>
      </div>

      <div class="module-card">
        <div class="module-card__number">Site 12</div>
        <div class="module-card__title">Baltimore Biotech Corridor</div>
        <p class="module-card__desc">
          Including Emergent BioSolutions. Pharmaceutical and biotech manufacturing,
          vaccine production, and public health infrastructure. What does it mean for
          a city to anchor a life-sciences industry?
        </p>
        <div class="module-card__sessions">Theme: Biotech · pharma · public health</div>
      </div>

    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Video Assignment Reminder ─────────────────────────────── -->
<section>
  <div class="container--text">
    <h2>Choosing your site</h2>
    <p>
      For the Baltimore Progress Video assignment, your group (of two or three) will choose one
      of the available sites from the list above. No two groups may select the same site.
      Sites labeled "Class visit" are reserved for course field trips and are not available
      for the video assignment. The goal is not to produce a tourist video — it is to use the
      site as a lens for thinking rigorously about progress.
    </p>
    <p>
      The best videos will find something surprising: a tension, a contradiction, an untold story,
      or an unexpected connection to the ideas we discuss in class. The site is a prompt
      for thinking, not a conclusion.
    </p>
    <p>
      Groups and sites are confirmed in the first week of class. The video is due in Week 4
      and screened in Week 5. The full assignment description is on
      the <a href="assignments.html">Assignments page</a>.
    </p>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Footer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
